neural-net-from-scratch.title: Neural Net from Scratch
neural-net-from-scratch.elevator: After this project, you'll understand exactly how neural networks learn — not as a black box, but every matrix multiply and gradient flow. You'll be able to read any PyTorch code and know what it's doing underneath.
neural-net-from-scratch.description: Build a complete neural network library from scratch in Python and NumPy — no PyTorch, no TensorFlow, no autograd. You implement Dense layers, multiple activation functions, loss functions, backpropagation via the chain rule, and optimizers from SGD to Adam. Then you train it on MNIST and prove it works.
neural-net-from-scratch.whyThisMatters: This project makes you write the code that every ML framework hides from you. Every concept in the next 17 projects rests on what you build here.
deliverables: A working neural network library supporting Dense layers, multiple activations (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, softmax), and optimizers (SGD, momentum, Adam)
Train on MNIST handwritten digits, achieve >95% accuracy
Gradient checking utility that verifies backprop implementation against numerical gradients
Training visualization: loss curves, accuracy curves, weight distribution histograms
A Jupyter notebook demonstrating the full training pipeline with explanations

steps: mkdir neural-net-from-scratch && cd neural-net-from-scratch
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install numpy matplotlib scikit-learn jupyter
git init && echo '.venv/\n__pycache__/\n.ipynb_checkpoints/' > .gitignore
Create the folder structure below and start with layers.py
setup.structure:
neural-net-from-scratch/
├── nn/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── layers.py          # Dense layer: forward, backward, params
│   ├── activations.py     # ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, softmax
│   ├── losses.py          # MSE, cross-entropy
│   ├── optimizers.py      # SGD, momentum, Adam
│   └── model.py           # Sequential model: forward, backward, train
├── notebooks/
│   ├── 01_xor_demo.ipynb  # Prove it works on XOR
│   └── 02_mnist.ipynb     # Full MNIST pipeline
├── tests/
│   ├── test_gradients.py  # Numerical gradient checking
│   └── test_shapes.py     # Verify tensor shapes through layers
├── .gitignore
└── README.md

papers.name: Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams 1986 — Learning representations by back-propagating errors
papers.why: The original paper that made neural networks trainable. Read sections 1-3 to understand the chain rule derivation.

papers.name: Kingma & Ba 2015 — Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
papers.why: The Adam optimizer paper. Read sections 1-2 for the algorithm, section 4 for convergence analysis.

videos.name: 3Blue1Brown: Neural Networks series (4 videos)
videos.why: Best visual intuition for what neural nets do geometrically — watch before writing any code

videos.name: Andrej Karpathy: Neural Networks Zero to Hero (first 3 videos)
videos.why: Builds exactly what you're building, step by step. The micrograd video is most relevant.

videos.name: 3Blue1Brown: Essence of Linear Algebra (full series)
videos.why: If linear algebra feels shaky, this series builds geometric intuition in ~3 hours

code.name: karpathy/micrograd
code.why: Tiny autograd engine in ~100 lines — the simplest possible implementation of backprop

code.name: numpy/numpy (source for broadcasting rules)
code.why: Understanding broadcasting is essential for eliminating loops in your implementation

books.name: Mathematics for Machine Learning — Deisenroth, Faisal, Ong
books.chapters: Ch. 2 (Linear Algebra), Ch. 3 (Geometry), Ch. 5 (Vector Calculus), Ch. 7 (Optimization)
books.why: The math foundations — read chapter 2 before starting, chapters 5 and 7 as you implement backprop and optimizers

books.name: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
books.why: The theoretical backbone — chapter 6 for architecture, chapter 8 for understanding why Adam works

milestones.name: Forward pass works
milestones.description: Implement Dense layer with weight initialization (He init for ReLU, Xavier for sigmoid/tanh). Implement ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, softmax activations. Build a Sequential model that chains layers.
milestones.criteria: model.forward(X) on random input produces output of correct shape. Softmax output sums to 1. Shapes verified for batch sizes 1, 32, 128.

milestones.name: Backprop works
milestones.description: Implement backward pass for every layer and activation. Build MSE and cross-entropy losses with gradients. Implement numerical gradient checking utility.
milestones.criteria: Gradient checking passes: analytical gradients match numerical gradients within 1e-5 relative error for all layer types and activations.

milestones.name: Training loop on XOR
milestones.description: Implement SGD optimizer. Build a training loop with batching. Train a 2-layer network on XOR (the classic non-linear test). Visualize the decision boundary.
milestones.criteria: XOR classification reaches 100% accuracy. Loss curve shows smooth convergence. Decision boundary plot shows non-linear separation.

milestones.name: Adam optimizer and regularization
milestones.description: Implement momentum SGD and Adam from the paper equations. Add L2 regularization and dropout. Compare SGD vs momentum vs Adam on a simple dataset.
milestones.criteria: Adam converges faster than SGD on MNIST subset. Dropout shows measurable reduction in overfitting on a small dataset.

milestones.name: MNIST >95% accuracy
milestones.description: Full pipeline: load MNIST, normalize, train with Adam, evaluate. Hyperparameter tuning: learning rate, hidden sizes, dropout rate. Produce a final notebook with training curves and confusion matrix.
milestones.criteria: Test accuracy exceeds 95% on MNIST. Training notebook is clean and documented. Confusion matrix identifies most-confused digit pairs.

neural-net-from-scratch.connects_to: This project's backpropagation implementation is the foundation for understanding PyTorch's autograd (Project 2). The optimization concepts (SGD, Adam) appear in every subsequent project. The linear algebra intuition you build here — matrix shapes, broadcasting, gradients as vectors — is the language of all deep learning.

cnn-image-classifier.title: CNN Image Classifier
cnn-image-classifier.elevator: After this project, you'll understand why convolutions are the right inductive bias for images, how to use PyTorch as a power user (not just copy-paste), and why GPUs transformed AI from academic curiosity to industrial revolution.
cnn-image-classifier.description: Build a convolutional neural network from scratch by extending your Project 1 library with Conv2D and pooling layers. Then reimplement the same architecture in PyTorch and compare. Fine-tune a pretrained ResNet on a custom dataset to see transfer learning in action.
cnn-image-classifier.whyThisMatters: This is your transition from pure math to real frameworks. You'll understand PyTorch deeply because you built the thing it automates.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU recommended (Google Colab free tier works). CPU works but training is 10-50x slower.

deliverables: Conv2D layer implemented from scratch in NumPy (forward and backward pass)
A PyTorch CNN trained on CIFAR-10, achieving >85% accuracy
Performance comparison: scratch implementation vs PyTorch (speed, accuracy, code complexity)
Transfer learning experiment: fine-tune pretrained ResNet-18 on a small custom dataset, achieve >90% accuracy with <100 images per class
Notebook explaining convolution arithmetic: how input size, kernel size, stride, and padding determine output size

steps: mkdir cnn-image-classifier && cd cnn-image-classifier
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch torchvision numpy matplotlib jupyter tensorboard
For GPU: pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
Alternatively: use Google Colab with GPU runtime (free)

papers.name: He et al. 2016 — Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
papers.why: ResNet introduced skip connections that enabled training 100+ layer networks. Read section 3 for the residual block design.

papers.name: LeCun et al. 1998 — Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition
papers.why: The original LeNet paper. Section 2 shows the first practical CNN architecture.

videos.name: Andrej Karpathy: CS231n Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition
videos.why: The definitive CNN course. Lectures 5 (ConvNets), 9 (Training), and 13 (Visualizing) are most relevant.

videos.name: PyTorch Beginner Tutorial Series (official)
videos.why: Learn PyTorch conventions before starting — tensors, autograd, nn.Module, DataLoader

code.name: pytorch/vision (torchvision models)
code.why: Read resnet.py to understand how production ResNet is implemented — surprisingly clean code

code.name: kuangliu/pytorch-cifar
code.why: Clean CIFAR-10 training pipeline with multiple architectures — good reference for training code

books.name: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
books.why: The theoretical treatment of convolutions, pooling, and why weight sharing works

books.name: Programming PyTorch for Deep Learning — Ian Pointer
books.why: Practical PyTorch patterns. Read chapter 3 for the training loop conventions.

milestones.name: Conv2D from scratch
milestones.description: Implement Conv2D forward pass in NumPy using nested loops first, then optimize with im2col. Implement backward pass for weight and input gradients.
milestones.criteria: Forward pass output matches PyTorch nn.Conv2d output within 1e-5. Backward pass gradients verified with numerical gradient checking.

milestones.name: PyTorch fundamentals
milestones.description: Reimplement your neural net in PyTorch. Learn autograd, nn.Module, DataLoader, optimizers. Train on MNIST using PyTorch to compare with Project 1.
milestones.criteria: MNIST accuracy matches or exceeds Project 1's accuracy. Can explain what autograd does at each step of the training loop.

milestones.name: CIFAR-10 CNN
milestones.description: Design and train a CNN on CIFAR-10. Experiment with depth, kernel sizes, batch normalization, data augmentation.
milestones.criteria: Test accuracy exceeds 85% on CIFAR-10. TensorBoard logs show training/validation curves.

milestones.name: Transfer learning with ResNet
milestones.description: Fine-tune a pretrained ResNet-18 on a small custom dataset (e.g., 5-10 classes, ~50-100 images each). Freeze early layers, retrain final classifier.
milestones.criteria: Achieves >90% accuracy on custom dataset with minimal data. Can explain which layers to freeze and why.

cnn-image-classifier.connects_to: PyTorch becomes your primary tool for every project from here on. The training loop patterns you learn here — DataLoader, optimizer.step(), loss.backward() — are identical in Projects 3-8. ResNet's skip connections reappear in the Transformer (Project 3). Transfer learning is the foundation of fine-tuning LLMs (Project 6).

transformer-from-scratch.title: Transformer from Scratch
transformer-from-scratch.elevator: After this project, you'll be able to read and understand the architecture of GPT, LLaMA, and every modern LLM. You'll know what attention actually computes and why transformers scaled where RNNs couldn't.
transformer-from-scratch.description: Build a complete transformer from scratch in PyTorch — scaled dot-product attention, multi-head attention, positional encoding, encoder blocks, decoder blocks, and the full architecture. Then train a character-level language model on Shakespeare (following Karpathy's nanoGPT approach) and generate coherent text.
transformer-from-scratch.whyThisMatters: The transformer is the architecture behind GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, and every frontier model. Building it from scratch makes you literate in the language that all of modern AI speaks.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU strongly recommended. Google Colab free tier (T4) works for character-level model. A100/H100 for anything larger.

deliverables: Complete transformer implementation: attention, multi-head attention, encoder block, decoder block, positional encoding
A trained character-level GPT on Shakespeare text that generates coherent prose
Attention visualization: heatmaps showing which tokens attend to which
Comparison of sinusoidal vs learned positional encodings
Written analysis: encoder-only (BERT) vs decoder-only (GPT) vs encoder-decoder (T5) — when to use which

steps: mkdir transformer-from-scratch && cd transformer-from-scratch
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch numpy matplotlib jupyter tiktoken wandb
Download Shakespeare dataset: wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt -O data/shakespeare.txt
Read Vaswani et al. 2017 sections 3.1-3.3 before starting

transformer-from-scratch/
├── model/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── attention.py        # Scaled dot-product, multi-head
│   ├── positional.py       # Sinusoidal, learned, RoPE
│   ├── encoder.py          # Encoder block: attn + FFN + residual
│   ├── decoder.py          # Decoder block: masked attn + cross-attn + FFN
│   ├── transformer.py      # Full encoder-decoder
│   └── gpt.py              # Decoder-only GPT for language modeling
├── data/
│   └── shakespeare.txt
├── train.py                # Training script with logging
├── generate.py             # Text generation with temperature sampling
├── notebooks/
│   ├── 01_attention_deep_dive.ipynb
│   ├── 02_train_char_gpt.ipynb
│   └── 03_attention_visualization.ipynb
├── .gitignore
└── README.md

papers.name: Vaswani et al. 2017 — Attention Is All You Need
papers.why: THE paper. Read every equation in section 3. Implement each one. This paper IS the project.

papers.name: Su et al. 2021 — RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
papers.why: RoPE is used in LLaMA, Mistral, and most modern LLMs. Read sections 3.1-3.3 for the rotation derivation.

papers.name: Dao et al. 2022 — FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
papers.why: Understanding why naive attention is memory-bound. Read section 2 for the problem, section 3 for the algorithm. You won't implement it, but understanding it matters.

videos.name: Andrej Karpathy: Let's build GPT: from scratch, in code, spelled out
videos.why: The single most relevant video. Builds exactly what you're building. Watch and code along.

videos.name: 3Blue1Brown: Attention in transformers, visually explained
videos.why: Visual intuition for what Q, K, V matrices actually compute geometrically

videos.name: Stanford CS224N: Lecture 8 — Self-Attention and Transformers
videos.why: Academic treatment with derivations. Watch after building to deepen understanding.

code.name: karpathy/nanoGPT
code.why: Clean GPT implementation in ~300 lines. Your target is to match this, understanding every line.

code.name: karpathy/minGPT
code.why: Slightly more structured than nanoGPT. Good for understanding the module decomposition.

books.name: Natural Language Processing with Transformers — Tunstall, von Werra, Wolf
books.why: Clear walkthrough of each component with HuggingFace code examples

books.name: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
books.chapters: Ch. 10 (Sequence Modeling) — for RNN context only
books.why: Understanding what transformers replaced and why attention was needed

milestones.name: Attention mechanism works
milestones.description: Implement scaled dot-product attention and multi-head attention. Verify shapes for different sequence lengths, batch sizes, and head counts. Implement causal masking for decoder attention.
milestones.criteria: Multi-head attention output shapes are correct. Causal mask prevents future token leakage (verified by checking attention weights). Output matches PyTorch nn.MultiheadAttention within tolerance.

milestones.name: Transformer blocks
milestones.description: Build encoder block (self-attention + FFN + LayerNorm + residual) and decoder block (masked self-attention + cross-attention + FFN). Implement sinusoidal positional encoding.
milestones.criteria: Can stack N encoder/decoder blocks. Residual connections verified: output = input + sublayer(input). LayerNorm statistics correct.

milestones.name: Character-level GPT trains
milestones.description: Build decoder-only GPT. Implement training loop with AdamW, learning rate warmup, and cosine decay. Train on Shakespeare text.
milestones.criteria: Training loss decreases smoothly. After 5000 steps, generated text shows recognizable English words and some Shakespearean patterns.

milestones.name: Generation and visualization
milestones.description: Implement temperature sampling, top-k, and nucleus (top-p) sampling. Build attention heatmap visualization for each layer and head.
milestones.criteria: Temperature=0 produces deterministic output. Higher temperatures increase diversity. Attention heatmaps show meaningful patterns (e.g., tokens attending to related tokens).

milestones.name: Architecture comparison write-up
milestones.description: Experiment with RoPE positional encoding. Write a comparison document: encoder-only vs decoder-only vs encoder-decoder, with concrete examples of when to use each.
milestones.criteria: RoPE implementation works. Document clearly explains the three architectures with real-world model examples.

transformer-from-scratch.connects_to: This is the architecture underlying everything from Project 5 onward. Pre-training (Project 5) trains this exact architecture at scale. Understanding attention is required for KV caching (Project 8), RAG reranking (Project 9), and agent reasoning (Project 12). RoPE is used in every open-source LLM you'll fine-tune in Project 6.

tokenizer-from-scratch.title: Tokenizer from Scratch
tokenizer-from-scratch.elevator: After this project, you'll understand why LLMs can't do arithmetic, why they're worse at Hindi than English, and why vocabulary size is a critical engineering decision — all because of tokenization.
tokenizer-from-scratch.description: Build a Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenizer from scratch. Then compare your implementation against OpenAI's tiktoken and Google's SentencePiece. Analyze how different tokenizers handle English text, Hindi text, code, and numbers to understand the real-world impact of tokenization choices.
tokenizer-from-scratch.whyThisMatters: Tokenization is the first and last thing that touches every LLM input and output. It silently determines what the model can and can't do well.
deliverables: A working BPE tokenizer built from scratch: training (merge learning) and encoding/decoding
Comparison analysis: your BPE vs tiktoken (GPT-4) vs SentencePiece (LLaMA) on identical text
Multilingual analysis: tokens per word in English, Hindi, Chinese, code — showing efficiency gaps
Arithmetic analysis: how tokenizers split numbers and why this makes math hard for LLMs
Vocabulary size experiment: train tokenizers at 1K, 8K, 32K, 100K vocab sizes and measure compression ratios

steps: mkdir tokenizer-from-scratch && cd tokenizer-from-scratch
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install tiktoken sentencepiece numpy matplotlib jupyter
Download a text corpus for training: e.g., a Wikipedia dump subset or Project Gutenberg books
Watch Karpathy's tokenizer video before starting

├── data/

│   └── 03_tokenization_effects.ipynb
├── .gitignore
└── README.md

papers.name: Sennrich, Haddow, Birch 2016 — Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units
papers.why: The paper that introduced BPE for NLP. Read sections 2-3 for the algorithm.

papers.name: Kudo & Richardson 2018 — SentencePiece: A simple and language independent subword tokenizer
papers.why: How SentencePiece works and why it handles multilingual text differently than BPE.

videos.name: Andrej Karpathy: Let's build the GPT Tokenizer
videos.why: Builds exactly what you're building. The most relevant resource for this project.

code.name: karpathy/minbpe
code.why: Minimal BPE implementation in ~100 lines. Your reference implementation.

code.name: openai/tiktoken
code.why: OpenAI's production tokenizer. Study the Python wrapper to understand the API design.

books.name: Natural Language Processing with Transformers — Tunstall, von Werra, Wolf
books.why: Practical overview of tokenizer types and their tradeoffs in production

milestones.name: BPE training works
milestones.description: Implement the BPE training algorithm: start with byte-level tokens, count pair frequencies, merge the most frequent pair, repeat for N merges. Save merge rules and vocabulary.
milestones.criteria: Can train a tokenizer on a text corpus. Vocabulary grows correctly with each merge. Merge rules are deterministic and reproducible.

milestones.name: Encode and decode
milestones.description: Implement encoding (text to token IDs) and decoding (token IDs to text). Handle edge cases: empty strings, unknown bytes, special tokens.
milestones.criteria: Round-trip: decode(encode(text)) == text for all test strings. Encoding produces the same output as the reference minbpe implementation.

milestones.name: Comparison analysis
milestones.description: Run the same text through your BPE, tiktoken (cl100k_base), and SentencePiece. Compare token counts, token boundaries, and compression ratios.
milestones.criteria: Comparison notebook with clear tables and visualizations. Can explain why each tokenizer makes different choices.

milestones.name: Impact analysis
milestones.description: Test multilingual efficiency (tokens per word across languages), arithmetic handling (how numbers are split), and code tokenization. Write analysis of how tokenization affects model behavior.
milestones.criteria: Quantified: Hindi uses N times more tokens than English for equivalent content. Clear explanation of why LLMs struggle with arithmetic due to tokenization.

tokenizer-from-scratch.connects_to: Tokenization decisions directly affect pre-training cost (Project 5) — more tokens = more compute. When fine-tuning (Project 6), you need to understand tokenizer compatibility. Inference cost (Project 8) is measured in tokens, so tokenizer efficiency = cost efficiency. RAG chunking (Project 9) must respect token boundaries.

pretrain-small-lm.title: Pre-train a Small LM
pretrain-small-lm.elevator: After this project, you'll understand the full pre-training pipeline — data processing, training at scale, scaling laws, and why a 125M model trained right outperforms a 1B model trained wrong. You'll speak the language of LLM training engineers.
pretrain-small-lm.description: Pre-train a 125M parameter language model on a subset of open data (e.g., FineWeb, RedPajama). You'll build the full pipeline: data processing, tokenization, model architecture, training with mixed precision and gradient checkpointing, and evaluation. You'll measure perplexity, analyze scaling behavior, and understand compute-optimal training.
pretrain-small-lm.whyThisMatters: Pre-training is the most expensive and consequential step in building an LLM. Understanding it — even at small scale — separates PMs who can set realistic expectations from those who treat models as magic.

tools: tiktoken or a custom tokenizer from Project 4
DeepSpeed or FSDP (for distributed training, optional)
prerequisites.hardware: GPU required. Minimum: 1x A100 40GB (or 2x T4 16GB). Recommended: 1x A100 80GB. Cloud options: Lambda Labs ($1.10/hr A100), RunPod, Google Colab Pro+.
prerequisites.time: 3-4 weekends (25-40 hours, including waiting for training runs)

deliverables: A trained 125M parameter language model with measured perplexity on a held-out validation set
Complete data pipeline: download, filter, deduplicate, tokenize, batch
Training curves: loss vs tokens, loss vs compute, learning rate schedule
Scaling analysis: train 10M, 50M, 125M models and plot loss vs model size (your own mini scaling law)
Cost analysis: total GPU hours, $/token, extrapolation to larger models

steps: mkdir pretrain-small-lm && cd pretrain-small-lm
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch transformers datasets tiktoken wandb accelerate
Set up GPU access: cloud provider account or local GPU
Download a data subset: huggingface-cli download HuggingFaceFW/fineweb --include 'sample/10BT/*' (10B token sample)
Read Hoffmann et al. 2022 (Chinchilla) before designing your training run

papers.name: Brown et al. 2020 — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
papers.why: The paper that showed scale is all you need. Read sections 2 (approach) and 4 (results) to understand the paradigm.

papers.name: Hoffmann et al. 2022 — Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla)
papers.why: The scaling laws paper. Proves GPT-3 was undertrained. Read section 3 for the compute-optimal ratio: ~20 tokens per parameter.

papers.name: Touvron et al. 2023 — LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
papers.why: Shows that smaller models + more data = better inference efficiency. Read section 2 for architecture choices.

papers.name: DeepSeek-AI 2024 — DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report
papers.why: State-of-the-art efficient pre-training. Sections on MoE, training stability, and data processing.

videos.name: Andrej Karpathy: Let's reproduce GPT-2 (124M)
videos.why: Exactly this project but done by Karpathy. Watch before or during your implementation.

videos.name: Stanford CS149: Parallel Computing (Lectures on GPU architecture)
videos.why: Understanding why training is compute-bound and how parallelism works

code.name: karpathy/nanoGPT
code.why: Your starting point. Study train.py closely — it handles mixed precision, gradient accumulation, DDP.

code.name: karpathy/llm.c
code.why: GPT-2 training in pure C/CUDA. Read for understanding the low-level operations.

code.name: huggingface/datatrove
code.why: Production data processing pipeline. Study for data filtering and deduplication patterns.

books.name: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
books.why: Learning rate schedules, batch size effects, gradient noise — all relevant to pre-training

books.name: Computer Organization and Design — Patterson & Hennessy
books.chapters: Ch. 6 (Parallel Processors from Client to Cloud)
books.why: Understanding the memory hierarchy and compute bottlenecks that determine training speed

milestones.name: Data pipeline ready
milestones.description: Download FineWeb sample, implement quality filtering (perplexity filter, language ID), deduplication (MinHash), and tokenization. Save as memory-mapped binary files for efficient loading.
milestones.criteria: Can load random batches of tokenized data instantly. Data statistics: total tokens, vocabulary coverage, average document length.

milestones.name: 10M model trains successfully
milestones.description: Train a 10M parameter model as a sanity check. Verify: loss decreases, no NaN/Inf, learning rate schedule works, checkpointing works. This should be fast (minutes, not hours).
milestones.criteria: 10M model training loss converges. Generated text is recognizable English. Validation perplexity is measured and logged.

milestones.name: Mixed precision and gradient checkpointing
milestones.description: Add BF16/FP16 mixed precision training. Implement gradient checkpointing to reduce memory. Add gradient accumulation for effective larger batch sizes.
milestones.criteria: Training speed increases ~2x with mixed precision. Memory usage decreases with gradient checkpointing. Training stability maintained (no loss spikes).

milestones.name: 125M model training run
milestones.description: Launch the full 125M model training run. Monitor with wandb. Apply Chinchilla-optimal data ratio. Run for the full compute budget.
milestones.criteria: 125M model reaches competitive perplexity (target: within 10% of nanoGPT reference). Training curves are smooth. Total training cost documented.

milestones.name: Scaling analysis
milestones.description: Compare 10M, 50M, 125M models. Plot loss vs model size, loss vs tokens. Extrapolate to predict 350M and 1B model performance. Write up scaling insights.
milestones.criteria: Scaling plot shows expected power-law relationship. Chinchilla-optimal token counts match your observations. Cost extrapolation document completed.

pretrain-small-lm.connects_to: Pre-training produces the base model that fine-tuning (Project 6) adapts. Understanding training dynamics helps you evaluate scaling decisions in the LLMs you'll use in Projects 9-14. The hardware concepts (memory hierarchy, roofline model) directly inform inference optimization (Project 8). Scaling laws are essential PM knowledge for evaluating model investment decisions.

finetune-llm.title: Fine-tune an LLM
finetune-llm.elevator: After this project, you'll be able to take any open-source LLM and adapt it to a specific domain — understanding exactly which method to use (full fine-tune vs LoRA vs QLoRA), how much data you need, and what quality looks like.
finetune-llm.description: Fine-tune an open-source LLM (LLaMA-3-8B or Mistral-7B) for a specific domain using LoRA and QLoRA. You'll prepare instruction data, apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning, evaluate against the base model, and understand when fine-tuning helps vs. when prompting is sufficient.
finetune-llm.whyThisMatters: Fine-tuning is how companies make generic LLMs useful for their specific domain. As a PM, you need to know when to fine-tune vs prompt vs RAG — this project gives you that judgment.

tools: Hugging Face Transformers, PEFT, TRL, Datasets
prerequisites.hardware: GPU required. QLoRA: 1x T4 16GB or A10 24GB. Full LoRA on 8B: 1x A100 40GB. Google Colab Pro works for QLoRA.

deliverables: A domain-specific fine-tuned model using LoRA on LLaMA-3-8B or Mistral-7B
Comparison: base model vs LoRA vs QLoRA on domain-specific evaluation set
Data preparation pipeline: instruction formatting, chat template application, quality filtering
Quantization comparison: FP16 vs INT8 vs INT4 quality metrics
A decision framework: when to fine-tune vs prompt-engineer vs RAG

milestones.description: Collect or curate 500-1000 high-quality instruction-response pairs for your chosen domain. Format using the model's chat template. Split into train/eval sets.
milestones.criteria: Dataset is clean, formatted correctly, and loadable by HuggingFace Datasets. Each example follows the correct chat template. Eval set has 50+ diverse examples.

milestones.name: LoRA training works

milestones.criteria: Training loss decreases. Model generates domain-relevant responses. wandb dashboard shows smooth training curves.

milestones.name: QLoRA comparison
milestones.description: Retrain with QLoRA (4-bit base model). Compare training speed, memory usage, and output quality against full LoRA.
milestones.criteria: QLoRA uses <50% memory of LoRA. Quality difference is documented (should be minimal for rank >= 16).

milestones.name: Evaluation and merge
milestones.description: Evaluate base model vs fine-tuned model on your domain eval set. Merge LoRA weights into base model for serving. Test the merged model.
milestones.criteria: Fine-tuned model measurably outperforms base model on domain tasks. Merged model produces identical outputs to LoRA model. Evaluation metrics documented.

finetune-llm.connects_to: The fine-tuned model from this project becomes the input to alignment (Project 7). LoRA math appears in every model adaptation discussion. alignment-lab.title: Alignment Lab
alignment-lab.elevator: After this project, you'll understand what makes ChatGPT 'feel different' from a base model, how preference optimization works, and why alignment is both the most important and most fragile part of the LLM pipeline.
alignment-lab.description: Implement DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) alignment training on the fine-tuned model from Project 6. Collect or curate preference data, train with DPO, and measure behavioral changes. Red-team your aligned model to find failure modes. Optionally implement a reward model + PPO pipeline for comparison.
alignment-lab.whyThisMatters: Alignment is what turns a next-token predictor into a helpful assistant. Understanding its mechanics — and its failure modes — is critical for any PM shipping AI products.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU required. Same requirements as Project 6. QLoRA DPO: 1x T4 16GB. Full: 1x A100 40GB.

Before/after comparison: same prompts, SFT vs DPO responses, rated by quality
Red-teaming report: 20+ adversarial prompts testing for jailbreaks, hallucination, and reward hacking
Optional: reward model trained on preferences, PPO pipeline for comparison with DPO

milestones.name: DPO training complete

llm-inference-optimizer.title: LLM Inference Optimizer
llm-inference-optimizer.elevator: After this project, you'll be able to evaluate any LLM serving configuration — understanding the tradeoffs between cost, latency, throughput, and quality. You'll know why inference is memory-bound and how to make it cheaper.
llm-inference-optimizer.description: Build a minimal LLM inference server with KV caching, quantization, and comprehensive benchmarking. Compare vLLM, TGI, and llama.cpp. Build a cost calculator for self-hosting vs API. Understand the hardware bottlenecks from transistors to tokens.
llm-inference-optimizer.whyThisMatters: Inference cost is the dominant cost of deploying LLMs. Every penny per token matters at scale. This project teaches you the engineering that turns a $100K/month inference bill into a $10K/month one.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU required for benchmarking. Minimum 1x T4 16GB. Ideal: test on multiple GPU types (T4, A10, A100) for comparison. llama.cpp runs on CPU/Apple Silicon too.

deliverables: Benchmark report comparing vLLM vs TGI vs llama.cpp: throughput (tokens/sec), latency (TTFT, TPS), memory usage
Quantization comparison: FP16 vs INT8 vs INT4 (GPTQ/AWQ/GGUF) — quality vs speed vs memory
KV cache implementation: from scratch in PyTorch showing memory growth and optimization
Cost calculator: spreadsheet/script that estimates $/1M tokens for any model + hardware + framework combo
The hardware story: a written walkthrough from sand to inference — semiconductors, GPU architecture, memory bandwidth, roofline model

rag-pipeline.title: RAG Pipeline
rag-pipeline.elevator: After this project, you'll be able to build a production-quality RAG system — the architecture behind every enterprise AI assistant including Moveworks. You'll know why naive RAG fails and how to make retrieval actually reliable.
rag-pipeline.description: Build a production-quality RAG system from scratch — not using LangChain as a black box. Implement the full pipeline: document ingestion, chunking, embedding, vector storage (Pinecone), retrieval (dense + sparse + hybrid), reranking, and LLM generation with citations. Evaluate rigorously with retrieval metrics and answer quality.
rag-pipeline.whyThisMatters: RAG is the dominant architecture for enterprise AI. Moveworks, every enterprise search product, and most production LLM applications use RAG. This is the project most directly relevant to your day job.

prerequisites.hardware: CPU sufficient for retrieval. GPU optional for local embedding and reranking models.

deliverables: Complete RAG pipeline: ingest -> chunk -> embed -> store -> retrieve -> rerank -> generate -> cite
Working system over a real document corpus (e.g., technical documentation, legal docs, or research papers)
Retrieval evaluation: recall@5, recall@10, MRR, nDCG on a labeled test set
Answer quality evaluation: factual accuracy, citation correctness, hallucination rate
Chunking experiment: compare fixed-size, recursive, and semantic chunking on the same corpus

Create a labeled evaluation set: 50+ questions with known correct answer passages

milestones.criteria: Generated answers include citations. Citation accuracy measured. Hallucination rate documented.

milestones.description: Build evaluation pipeline with retrieval metrics (recall@k, MRR, nDCG) and answer quality metrics (faithfulness, relevancy). Run on 50+ test questions.
milestones.criteria: Full evaluation report. Baseline metrics established. Each component's contribution measured (ablation study).

The entire Moveworks product stack relies on RAG architecture.

advanced-rag.title: Advanced RAG
advanced-rag.elevator: After this project, you'll know when basic RAG fails and which advanced technique to reach for — GraphRAG for summarization queries, Self-RAG for reliability, HyDE for query-document mismatch, and long-context for simplicity.
advanced-rag.description: Extend your RAG pipeline with advanced techniques: HyDE (hypothetical document embeddings), Self-RAG and Corrective RAG (CRAG), GraphRAG with knowledge graphs, and multi-modal RAG. Compare all approaches against basic RAG and long-context on the same benchmark.
advanced-rag.whyThisMatters: Basic RAG fails in predictable ways. This project teaches you to diagnose failure modes and select the right technique for each situation — essential for building reliable AI products.

prerequisites.hardware: CPU sufficient for most. GPU helpful for local embedding models. Long-context experiments need API access to 100K+ context models.

deliverables: HyDE implementation with comparison against direct embedding search

GraphRAG: knowledge graph extraction, community detection, hierarchical summarization
Side-by-side benchmark: basic RAG vs HyDE vs Self-RAG vs GraphRAG vs long-context on 50+ questions
Decision framework: when to use which RAG technique, with concrete criteria

milestones.name: HyDE implementation
milestones.description: Implement HyDE: for a query, generate a hypothetical answer, embed that answer, and search for real documents similar to it. Compare against direct query embedding.
milestones.criteria: HyDE improves recall on queries where the query and document use different vocabulary. Documented comparison with direct embedding search.

milestones.name: Self-RAG / CRAG
milestones.description: Implement a system where the LLM decides whether to retrieve (based on query complexity), critiques retrieved documents for relevance, and evaluates its own output for support.
milestones.criteria: System skips retrieval for simple factual questions. Retrieves for knowledge-intensive questions. Self-critique catches at least some unsupported claims.

milestones.name: GraphRAG
milestones.description: Extract entities and relationships from documents using an LLM. Build a knowledge graph. Detect communities. Generate hierarchical summaries. Answer questions using the graph.
milestones.criteria: Knowledge graph has meaningful entities and relationships. GraphRAG answers summarization questions that basic RAG cannot.

milestones.name: Comprehensive benchmark
milestones.description: Run all techniques (basic RAG, HyDE, Self-RAG, GraphRAG, long-context stuffing) on the same 50+ question benchmark. Categorize questions by type (factual, summarization, multi-hop, comparison).
milestones.criteria: Clear comparison table. Each technique has documented strengths and weaknesses. Decision framework: which technique for which query type.

advanced-rag.connects_to: GraphRAG and advanced retrieval patterns are directly applicable to Enterprise Search (Project 11). enterprise-search-engine.title: Enterprise Search Engine
enterprise-search-engine.elevator: After this project, you'll have built a working prototype of what Moveworks, Glean, and other enterprise search products do — multi-source ingestion, hybrid search, access control, and AI-powered answers. This is the project most relevant to your career.
enterprise-search-engine.description: Build a full enterprise search system: connectors that ingest from multiple data sources, hybrid search (BM25 + semantic), access control and multi-tenancy, and AI-powered answer generation. This ties together RAG, embeddings, and enterprise patterns into a production-like system.
enterprise-search-engine.whyThisMatters: This is your job. Building this from scratch gives you the deepest possible understanding of what Moveworks does, why certain architectural decisions are made, and what the hard problems actually are.

prerequisites.hardware: CPU sufficient. Local Elasticsearch or Meilisearch instance. Cloud vector DB (Pinecone).

deliverables: Working enterprise search engine with at least 3 data source connectors (e.g., Confluence, Google Drive, Notion or local file system)
Hybrid search: BM25 + semantic with RRF fusion, reranking, and AI-generated answers
Access control: per-user and per-group permissions enforced at query time
Multi-tenancy: complete data isolation between tenants
Search quality evaluation: relevance metrics on a labeled test set
Admin dashboard: connector status, index statistics, query analytics

Set up Elasticsearch locally: docker run -d -p 9200:9200 elasticsearch:8.11.0
Set up Pinecone index for semantic search
Design your data sources: pick 3 sources you can programmatically access

milestones.name: Connector framework
milestones.description: Build the abstract connector interface and implement 2 connectors (e.g., local filesystem + one API-based source). Implement incremental sync: detect new/modified/deleted documents.
milestones.criteria: Connectors run, ingest documents, and store metadata. Incremental sync only processes changed documents. Connector status tracked in metadata DB.

milestones.name: Hybrid search works
milestones.description: Index documents in both Elasticsearch (BM25) and Pinecone (semantic). Implement hybrid retrieval with RRF fusion. Add cross-encoder reranking.
milestones.criteria: Hybrid search returns relevant results for both keyword and semantic queries. Hybrid beats either individual method on test queries.

milestones.name: Access control and multi-tenancy
milestones.description: Implement user/group permission model. Enforce ACLs at query time (pre-filtering). Implement tenant isolation using namespace separation.
milestones.criteria: User A cannot see User B's documents. Tenant isolation is complete — no cross-tenant leaks. ACL enforcement tested with multiple user scenarios.

milestones.name: API and AI answers
milestones.description: Build FastAPI endpoints for search, admin, and connector management. Add AI-generated answers with citations from retrieved documents.
milestones.criteria: Full API working: search endpoint returns results with AI-generated answer. Admin endpoints show connector status and index stats.

milestones.name: Evaluation and analytics
milestones.description: Create a labeled test set of 30+ queries with expected documents. Evaluate search quality. Build basic analytics: query volume, click-through rate simulation, result relevance.
milestones.criteria: Search quality metrics documented. Analytics dashboard shows query patterns. Areas for improvement identified.

enterprise-search-engine.connects_to: This project is the most direct application of everything you've learned to your Moveworks work. The connector, search, and ACL patterns are exactly what enterprise AI products use. The architecture decisions transfer directly to understanding your product's technical stack. The evaluation patterns extend to Project 14 (AI Product Simulator).

ai-agent-from-scratch.title: AI Agent from Scratch
ai-agent-from-scratch.elevator: After this project, you'll understand the difference between a chatbot and an agent, how to build reliable tool-using systems, and why agent reliability math is the single most important concept in agentic AI.
ai-agent-from-scratch.description: Build an AI agent with tool use, planning, and memory — without frameworks first, then reimplemented with LangGraph. The agent should research topics, use tools (search, calculator, code execution), maintain memory across sessions, and produce structured reports. Focus on reliability and error handling.
ai-agent-from-scratch.whyThisMatters: Agentic AI is the next frontier for enterprise products. Understanding how agents work at the implementation level — especially the reliability challenges — is essential for building and managing agent-based products.

prerequisites.hardware: CPU sufficient. LLM API access required. Docker for sandboxed execution.

deliverables: A working AI agent built from scratch: ReAct loop, tool calling, error recovery
Same agent reimplemented in LangGraph for comparison
Tool suite: web search, calculator, code execution (sandboxed), file I/O
Memory system: short-term (conversation), long-term (vector DB), working (scratchpad)
Reliability analysis: measure success rate over 50+ tasks, identify failure modes
Structured output: agent produces formatted reports with sources

Set up API keys for your LLM provider

milestones.name: ReAct loop from scratch
milestones.description: Build the core agent loop: parse the LLM's decision to think or act, execute tools, feed observations back. Handle tool errors gracefully. Implement max-step limits.
milestones.criteria: Agent can solve simple tasks (search + summarize) using the ReAct pattern. Tool errors don't crash the agent. Conversation history is maintained.

milestones.name: Tool suite
milestones.description: Build 4+ tools: web search (via API), calculator (safe math eval), sandboxed code execution (Docker), file I/O. Each tool has clear input/output schemas.
milestones.criteria: All tools work independently. Code execution is sandboxed (no filesystem or network access). Tools handle edge cases gracefully.

milestones.name: Memory system
milestones.description: Implement three memory tiers: short-term (recent messages with truncation), long-term (facts stored in Pinecone, retrieved by relevance), working memory (scratchpad for the current task).
milestones.criteria: Agent remembers facts across sessions using long-term memory. Working memory helps with multi-step reasoning. Context window management prevents overflow.

milestones.name: LangGraph reimplementation
milestones.description: Rebuild the same agent in LangGraph. Define the graph: nodes for reasoning, action, observation. Add human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions.
milestones.criteria: LangGraph agent produces equivalent outputs to the scratch agent. Human-in-the-loop works for designated actions. Persistence works across sessions.

milestones.name: Reliability evaluation
milestones.description: Create 50+ evaluation tasks across categories (research, calculation, code, multi-step). Run both agents. Measure success rate, failure modes, and cost per task.
milestones.criteria: Reliability report with success rates by task category. Failure mode taxonomy (tool errors, reasoning errors, hallucination). Cost analysis per task.

ai-agent-from-scratch.connects_to: Agent architecture is the foundation for multi-agent systems (Project 13) and the full enterprise AI product (Project 14). The reliability math — understanding that per-step accuracy compounds exponentially — is the most important insight for any PM building agentic products. Memory patterns reappear in every agent-based system.

multi-agent-system.title: Multi-Agent System
multi-agent-system.elevator: After this project, you'll understand when to use a single agent vs. multiple specialized agents, how to orchestrate agent collaboration, and the safety patterns that prevent multi-agent systems from going off the rails.
multi-agent-system.description: Build a multi-agent system where specialized agents collaborate on complex tasks. Implement supervisor/worker patterns, agent delegation, shared state, and human-in-the-loop approval flows. Compare LangGraph, CrewAI, and the Claude Agent SDK as orchestration frameworks.
multi-agent-system.whyThisMatters: Enterprise AI products increasingly use multi-agent architectures. Understanding orchestration patterns — and their failure modes — is essential for designing reliable agent-based products like Moveworks.

prerequisites.hardware: CPU sufficient. LLM API access required. Budget ~$20-50 in API costs for multi-agent experiments.

deliverables: A multi-agent system with supervisor + 3 specialized worker agents
Concrete workflow: e.g., research -> analysis -> report generation, or code review pipeline
Framework comparison: LangGraph vs CrewAI vs Claude Agent SDK on the same workflow
Safety layer: approval gates, output validation, cost limits, timeout handling
Evaluation: success rate, latency, cost, and quality compared to single-agent approach

milestones.name: Supervisor + workers pattern
milestones.description: Build a supervisor agent that receives a complex task, decomposes it into subtasks, delegates to specialist workers, and aggregates results. Workers have distinct tools and prompts.
milestones.criteria: Supervisor successfully decomposes and delegates tasks. Workers produce specialist outputs. Final aggregated result is coherent.

milestones.name: Safety and approval gates
milestones.description: Add human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions. Implement cost limits, timeout handling, and output validation. Test failure recovery.
milestones.criteria: High-impact actions pause for human approval. Cost limits prevent runaway spending. Timeouts prevent infinite loops. Agent recovers gracefully from worker failures.

milestones.name: Framework comparison
milestones.description: Implement the same workflow in LangGraph, CrewAI, and Claude Agent SDK. Compare code complexity, flexibility, reliability, and observability.
milestones.criteria: Same workflow works in all three frameworks. Comparison document with clear trade-offs. Recommendation for when to use each.

milestones.name: Evaluation: single vs multi-agent
milestones.description: Run the same complex tasks with a single powerful agent and your multi-agent system. Compare success rate, quality, latency, and cost.
milestones.criteria: Quantified comparison across 20+ tasks. Clear analysis of when multi-agent adds value vs. overhead. Cost-per-task breakdown.

multi-agent-system.connects_to: Multi-agent patterns are directly relevant to enterprise AI products (Project 14) where different agents handle different capabilities (IT support, HR queries, etc.). The safety patterns — approval gates, guardrails, cost limits — are essential for any production agent deployment. Framework evaluation knowledge helps you make build-vs-buy decisions as a PM.

ai-product-simulator.connects_to: This project is the capstone of your practical AI engineering journey. Every concept converges here: neural networks (Project 1) power the models, transformers (Project 3) are the architecture, fine-tuning (Project 6) adapts them, inference optimization (Project 8) makes them affordable, RAG (Projects 9-11) grounds them, and agents (Projects 12-13) make them useful. From here, Project 15 (Autonomous Research Agent) pushes agentic AI to its limit, then you move to frontier research (Projects 16-19).

autonomous-research-agent.title: Autonomous Research Agent
autonomous-research-agent.elevator: After this project, you'll have built an AI agent that designs its own ML experiments, runs them overnight, evaluates results, and iterates — the closest thing to an autonomous AI researcher. Inspired by Karpathy's autoresearch.
autonomous-research-agent.description: Build an autonomous research agent that takes a training script and a goal (e.g., 'minimize val loss on CIFAR-10'), then independently runs experiments: modifying hyperparameters, tweaking architecture, evaluating results, and deciding what to try next. It combines everything from your agentic phase — tool use, code generation, multi-step reasoning — with your ML training knowledge. The agent runs N experiments over several hours, keeps a structured log, and produces a final research report summarizing what worked and why.
autonomous-research-agent.whyThisMatters: This is the ultimate test of agentic AI: an agent that doesn't just chat or retrieve documents, but actually does science. It closes the loop between LLM reasoning and real-world experimentation, which is where the industry is heading with AI scientists and automated research.

tools: An LLM API (Anthropic Claude or OpenAI GPT-4)
A small GPU (T4 or better — Colab Pro works)
prerequisites.hardware: GPU required for running training experiments. T4 16GB minimum. LLM API access required (~$10-20 in API costs).

deliverables: An experiment runner that executes a training script, captures metrics (loss, accuracy, training time), and stores structured results
A code modification agent that reads a training script, proposes changes (hyperparameters, architecture, regularization), applies them via function calling, and runs the modified experiment
An evaluation and decision loop: compare experiments, keep improvements, revert regressions, decide what to try next
A full overnight run pipeline: N experiments executed autonomously with structured logging of every change and result
An auto-generated research report summarizing which modifications helped most, the best configuration found, and lessons learned

milestones.name: Experiment runner
milestones.description: Build infrastructure to run a training script as a subprocess, capture stdout and metrics (loss, accuracy per epoch), store results in a structured format (JSONL). The agent doesn't modify anything yet — just runs and records.
milestones.criteria: Runner executes baseline train.py, captures per-epoch loss and accuracy, stores results in experiments/results.jsonl with timestamp, config hash, and metrics. Handles script crashes gracefully.

milestones.name: Code modification agent
milestones.description: Build an agent that reads train.py, proposes a modification (change learning rate, add dropout, change hidden size), applies it using LLM function calling, and runs the modified experiment. The agent sees the code and recent results.
milestones.criteria: Agent successfully proposes and applies at least 3 different types of modifications (hyperparameter, architecture, regularization). Modified scripts run without errors. Changes are logged with diffs.

milestones.name: Evaluation and decision loop
milestones.description: After each experiment, the agent evaluates: did val loss improve? If yes, keep the change as the new baseline. If no, revert. Build the autonomous loop with a configurable number of iterations.
milestones.criteria: Agent correctly identifies improvements vs regressions. Reverts bad changes. Keeps a running 'best configuration' that improves over 5+ iterations. Decision reasoning is logged.

milestones.name: Overnight autonomous run
milestones.description: Set up the full pipeline: the agent runs N experiments (e.g., 20-50) over several hours. Configure time and compute budgets. The agent manages its own experiment queue, avoids repeating failed approaches, and explores systematically.
milestones.criteria: Agent completes 20+ experiments autonomously. Final best model outperforms baseline. Experiment log shows coherent exploration strategy (not random). No human intervention needed after start.

milestones.name: Research report generation
milestones.description: The agent generates a markdown report: summary of all experiments, which changes helped most, learning curves, the final best configuration, and recommendations for further research.
milestones.criteria: Report includes: experiment summary table, top 5 most impactful changes, loss curves comparison, final config vs baseline, and actionable next steps. Report is readable and accurate.

autonomous-research-agent.connects_to: This project is the ultimate capstone of the agentic phase: it takes the agent framework from Project 12, the multi-agent patterns from Project 13, and the ML training knowledge from Project 5, and pushes them to their limit. The autonomous experimentation loop is also a preview of where AI research itself is heading — from Projects 16-19 (frontier research), you'll see the models that agents like this one will eventually improve.

diffusion-and-generation.title: Diffusion & Generation
diffusion-and-generation.elevator: After this project, you'll understand how Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Midjourney work — from the mathematical foundations of diffusion processes to the U-Net architecture that powers image generation.
diffusion-and-generation.description: Build a diffusion model from scratch. Start with a simple 2D diffusion (denoising spirals), then scale to image generation. Implement the forward diffusion process (adding noise), the reverse process (denoising network), and the sampling procedure. Study the Stable Diffusion architecture.
diffusion-and-generation.whyThisMatters: Generative AI is not just text. Understanding diffusion models — the foundation of image generation — completes your understanding of the generative AI landscape.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU required. Minimum 1x T4 16GB. A100 for full image diffusion. Colab Pro works.

deliverables: 2D diffusion model from scratch: denoise spiral data, visualize the forward and reverse process
Image diffusion model: train a simple DDPM on MNIST or CIFAR-10
Stable Diffusion exploration: use the Diffusers library to understand text-to-image, inpainting, and ControlNet
Written comparison: diffusion vs GANs vs VAEs — strengths, weaknesses, use cases
Visualizations: the denoising process at each timestep, latent space interpolation

steps: mkdir diffusion-and-generation && cd diffusion-and-generation
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch torchvision diffusers transformers accelerate matplotlib jupyter
For full Stable Diffusion: need ~16GB GPU memory (Colab Pro with A100)
Read Ho et al. 2020 sections 1-3 before starting

papers.name: Ho, Jain, Abbeel 2020 — Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)
papers.why: The foundational diffusion paper. Read sections 1-4 for the full derivation.

papers.name: Rombach et al. 2022 — High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
papers.why: The Stable Diffusion paper. Read section 3 for the latent space trick that made high-res diffusion practical.

papers.name: Song et al. 2020 — Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM)
papers.why: Faster sampling (50 steps vs 1000). Read section 4 for the non-Markovian sampling.

videos.name: Outlier: Diffusion models explained (full series)
videos.why: Visual explanation of the math behind diffusion models

videos.name: Yannic Kilcher: DDPM paper explained
videos.why: Detailed paper walkthrough with math explanations

code.name: huggingface/diffusers
code.why: Production diffusion model library. Study src/diffusers/models/ for architecture patterns.

code.name: lucidrains/denoising-diffusion-pytorch
code.why: Clean, readable diffusion implementation. Good reference for your from-scratch build.

books.name: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
books.why: Theoretical foundation for generative models: VAEs, GANs, and the concepts that led to diffusion

milestones.name: 2D diffusion from scratch
milestones.description: Implement forward process (adding Gaussian noise at each timestep) and a simple MLP that learns to reverse it. Train on 2D spiral data. Visualize the denoising process.
milestones.criteria: Model generates new 2D points that follow the spiral distribution. Forward/reverse process visualized clearly.

milestones.name: DDPM on images
milestones.description: Implement a U-Net architecture. Train DDPM on MNIST (simpler) or CIFAR-10 (harder). Implement linear and cosine noise schedules.
milestones.criteria: Generated images are recognizable digits/objects. Training loss decreases. FID score measured (if feasible).

milestones.name: Sampling methods
milestones.description: Implement DDPM sampling (1000 steps) and DDIM sampling (50 steps). Compare quality and speed.
milestones.criteria: DDIM produces comparable quality in 20x fewer steps. Speed comparison documented.

milestones.name: Stable Diffusion exploration
milestones.description: Use Diffusers library for text-to-image, image-to-image, inpainting, and ControlNet. Analyze the latent space architecture.
milestones.criteria: Working examples of each capability. Written explanation of the VAE encoder -> U-Net -> VAE decoder pipeline.

diffusion-and-generation.connects_to: Diffusion models represent a fundamentally different approach to generation than autoregressive models (GPT). Understanding both gives you a complete picture of generative AI. Multi-modal models (like GPT-4V) combine these approaches. The U-Net architecture influences modern vision-language models.

mamba-and-alternatives.title: Mamba & Alternatives
mamba-and-alternatives.elevator: After this project, you'll understand the fundamental limitations of transformers (quadratic attention cost) and the alternative architectures — state space models, Mamba, RWKV — that aim to solve them. You'll have informed opinions on what comes after transformers.
mamba-and-alternatives.description: Study and implement state space models (S4, Mamba) as alternatives to transformers. Understand the mathematical foundations (continuous-time systems, discretization), implement a simplified Mamba block, and compare against a transformer on the same task. Analyze the tradeoffs: what SSMs do better, and where transformers still win.
mamba-and-alternatives.whyThisMatters: The transformer may not be the final architecture. Understanding alternatives — especially where they win (long sequences, linear scaling) and lose (precise retrieval) — prepares you for the next paradigm shift.

prerequisites.hardware: GPU recommended for training comparisons. CPU works for understanding and small experiments.

deliverables: Simplified S4/Mamba block implementation in PyTorch
Side-by-side comparison: Mamba vs Transformer on language modeling (perplexity, speed, memory)
Analysis of where SSMs win (long sequences, streaming) and where they lose (in-context retrieval)
Written survey: Mamba, RWKV, Hyena, and hybrid architectures (Jamba, StripedHyena)
Scaling analysis: memory and compute vs sequence length for attention vs SSM

steps: mkdir mamba-and-alternatives && cd mamba-and-alternatives
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch mamba-ssm causal-conv1d numpy matplotlib jupyter
Read Gu & Dao 2023 (Mamba) before starting
Review linear algebra: diagonalization, eigendecomposition

papers.name: Gu & Dao 2023 — Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces
papers.why: The Mamba paper. Read sections 2-3 for the selection mechanism that makes SSMs input-dependent.

papers.name: Gu et al. 2021 — Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces (S4)
papers.why: The S4 paper that started the SSM revolution. Read section 3 for the HiPPO initialization.

papers.name: Peng et al. 2023 — RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era
papers.why: An alternative RNN-like architecture with transformer-like training. Section 3 for the WKV mechanism.

videos.name: Albert Gu: Mamba and State Space Models (talk)
videos.why: The creator explains the motivation and design decisions

videos.name: Yannic Kilcher: Mamba paper explained
videos.why: Detailed paper walkthrough with good intuition for the selection mechanism

code.why: Official Mamba implementation. Study mamba_ssm/modules/mamba_simple.py for the core block.

code.name: state-spaces/s4
code.why: Original S4 implementation. Study for the continuous-time formulation.

books.name: Probabilistic Machine Learning Vol. 2 — Murphy
books.why: Mathematical foundations of state space models from a probabilistic perspective

milestones.name: SSM mathematical foundations
milestones.description: Understand the continuous-time SSM (dx/dt = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du), discretization, and the HiPPO initialization. Implement a simplified S4 block.
milestones.criteria: Can explain the connection between continuous-time systems and discrete sequence models. Simplified S4 block runs on synthetic sequences.

milestones.name: Mamba implementation
milestones.description: Implement the Mamba block with the selection mechanism (input-dependent A, B, C parameters). Understand why this makes SSMs content-aware.
milestones.criteria: Mamba block produces correct output shapes. Selection mechanism visually demonstrated: different inputs produce different state dynamics.

milestones.name: Transformer vs Mamba comparison
milestones.description: Train both architectures (same parameter count) on a language modeling task. Compare: perplexity, training speed, inference speed, memory usage across sequence lengths.
milestones.criteria: Comparison table with clear metrics. Mamba shows linear scaling advantage on long sequences. Quality comparison at standard sequence lengths.

milestones.name: Architecture survey
milestones.description: Write a survey of alternative architectures: Mamba, RWKV, Hyena, and hybrid models (Jamba). Analyze the tradeoffs and predict which approach will win.
milestones.criteria: Written document with clear comparison. Retrieval test shows where SSMs lag. Informed prediction about future architectures.

mamba-and-alternatives.connects_to: Understanding alternative architectures helps you evaluate new models as a PM. When a new architecture claims superiority over transformers, you'll be able to assess the claim. Hybrid architectures (combining attention and SSMs) are emerging in production models — knowing both helps you understand them.

reasoning-models.title: Reasoning Models
reasoning-models.elevator: After this project, you'll understand how models like o1 and o3 think before answering — the chain-of-thought internalization, process reward models, and test-time compute scaling that represents the current frontier of AI capability.
reasoning-models.description: Explore the reasoning model paradigm: chain-of-thought prompting and internalization, process reward models (PRMs) vs outcome reward models (ORMs), and test-time compute scaling. Implement a simple process reward model, experiment with chain-of-thought fine-tuning, and analyze the tradeoffs of thinking longer vs using larger models.
reasoning-models.whyThisMatters: Reasoning models represent the newest and most impactful paradigm in AI. Understanding how 'thinking' works — and its cost implications — is critical for any PM evaluating model capabilities.

tools: Anthropic API (for Claude reasoning) or OpenAI API (for o1/o3)
prerequisites.hardware: GPU recommended for training experiments. API access for frontier reasoning models.

deliverables: Analysis of chain-of-thought prompting: when it helps, when it hurts, how it scales with problem difficulty
Process reward model implementation: score intermediate reasoning steps, not just final answers
Test-time compute experiment: measure accuracy vs thinking time on math/logic problems
Comparison: small model + long thinking vs large model + short thinking on benchmark problems
Written analysis: the economics of test-time compute and implications for AI products

python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch transformers trl anthropic openai wandb jupyter
Prepare a math/logic benchmark: GSM8K, MATH, or ARC
Read Wei et al. 2022 (Chain-of-Thought) and Lightman et al. 2023 (PRM) before starting

papers.name: Wei et al. 2022 — Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.why: The original CoT paper. Read section 3 for the key finding: CoT only helps models above a certain scale.

papers.name: Lightman et al. 2023 — Let's Verify Step by Step
papers.why: Process reward models from OpenAI. Section 4 shows PRMs significantly outperform ORMs for math reasoning.

papers.name: Snell et al. 2024 — Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally
papers.why: The test-time compute scaling paper. Shows when thinking longer helps and when it doesn't.

videos.name: OpenAI: Introducing o1 (technical overview)
videos.why: OpenAI's explanation of how reasoning models work

videos.name: Yannic Kilcher: Chain-of-Thought and reasoning papers
videos.why: Paper walkthroughs for the key reasoning papers

code.name: openai/prm800k
code.why: OpenAI's process reward model dataset. Study the step-level annotations.

books.name: Probabilistic Machine Learning Vol. 2 — Murphy
books.chapters: Ch. 8 (Monte Carlo Methods) — for understanding best-of-N sampling
books.why: The mathematical foundation for sampling-based reasoning strategies

milestones.name: Chain-of-thought analysis
milestones.description: Run CoT prompting experiments on GSM8K math problems. Compare: no CoT, zero-shot CoT ('think step by step'), and few-shot CoT. Measure accuracy and token usage across problem difficulties.
milestones.criteria: Clear accuracy comparison across prompting strategies. Analysis of when CoT helps (harder problems) vs hurts (simple problems, added cost).

milestones.name: Process reward model
milestones.description: Train a simple PRM: given a math solution with step annotations, predict which steps are correct. Use the PRM800K dataset or create a smaller annotated set.
milestones.criteria: PRM can distinguish correct from incorrect reasoning steps. Accuracy on held-out step annotations. Comparison with outcome-only reward model.

milestones.name: Test-time compute scaling
milestones.description: Implement best-of-N sampling: generate N solutions, score with PRM, select the best. Vary N (1, 4, 16, 64) and measure accuracy vs compute. Compare with beam search over reasoning steps.
milestones.criteria: Accuracy improves with N (diminishing returns). Cost-accuracy tradeoff curve plotted. Break-even point identified: when more thinking beats a bigger model.

milestones.name: Economics analysis
milestones.description: Write an analysis of test-time compute economics: if a small model thinking 10x longer matches a large model, when is each approach cheaper? Factor in latency, throughput, and cost per token.
milestones.criteria: Clear economic analysis with specific numbers. Decision framework: when to use reasoning models vs standard models for different use cases.

reasoning-models.connects_to: Reasoning model capabilities directly affect agent design (Projects 12-13) — agents with better reasoning need fewer tool calls. The economic analysis is essential PM knowledge for model selection in AI products. Process reward models extend alignment concepts (Project 7) from final outputs to intermediate reasoning.

open-source-contribution.title: Open Source Contribution
open-source-contribution.elevator: After this project, you'll have contributed to a production AI codebase used by thousands of engineers, proven you can read and improve code at scale, and built credibility in the open-source AI community.
open-source-contribution.description: Contribute to a major open-source AI project — applying everything you've learned across the entire journey. Start with documentation fixes and bug reports, graduate to bug fixes and small features. Target projects you've used throughout this journey: Transformers, vLLM, LangChain/LangGraph, or PEFT.
open-source-contribution.whyThisMatters: Reading and contributing to production AI code is the ultimate test of your understanding. It also builds credibility — open-source contributions are visible proof of technical depth.

tools: The development environment for your chosen project
Docker (for running tests in some projects)
prerequisites.hardware: Depends on the project. GPU may be needed for running tests on ML projects.
prerequisites.time: Ongoing (start with 2-3 weekends for first PR, then continue as a habit)

deliverables: At least 1 merged pull request to a major AI open-source project
3+ filed issues (bug reports, documentation gaps, feature requests) with clear reproduction steps
Development environment set up for 2+ major AI projects
Code review notes: what you learned from reading the codebase
A personal contribution plan: which projects, what areas, long-term goals

steps: mkdir open-source-contribution && cd open-source-contribution
Choose your target project (recommendation: start with huggingface/transformers — active, welcoming community)
Fork the repository and set up the development environment
Read CONTRIBUTING.md and the developer guide thoroughly
Run the test suite locally to verify your setup
Browse 'good first issue' labels and recent discussions

│   └── pr_log.md            # Log of your contributions
├── patches/
│   └── (local patch files for work in progress)
├── .gitignore
└── README.md

# Actual contribution work happens in forked repos:
# ~/repos/transformers/      (your fork)
# ~/repos/vllm/              (your fork)
# etc.

videos.name: How to Contribute to Open Source (GitHub guide)
videos.why: The definitive guide to open-source contribution etiquette and workflow

code.name: huggingface/transformers
code.why: Most welcoming community, clear CONTRIBUTING.md, 'good first issue' labels. Start here.

code.name: vllm-project/vllm
code.why: Inference serving. More systems-level. Good if you want to contribute to performance-critical code.

code.name: langchain-ai/langgraph
code.why: Agent framework. Good for contributing agent patterns and examples.

code.name: huggingface/peft
code.why: PEFT methods library. Smaller codebase, easier to understand fully.

books.name: Working in Public: The Making and Maintenance of Open Source Software — Nadia Eghbal
books.why: Understanding open-source community dynamics, maintainer perspectives, and how to be a good contributor

milestones.name: Environment setup and codebase reading
milestones.description: Fork, clone, set up development environment, run tests. Read the project architecture: entry points, core abstractions, test structure. Take notes on what you learn.
milestones.criteria: Development environment works. Tests pass locally. Architecture notes document: main modules, key abstractions, code style conventions.

milestones.name: First issues filed
milestones.description: File 2-3 well-written issues: documentation gaps, bugs you've encountered, or improvement suggestions. Each with clear description, reproduction steps, and expected behavior.
milestones.criteria: Issues are clear, well-formatted, and get responses from maintainers. Not duplicates of existing issues.

milestones.name: First PR submitted
milestones.description: Pick a 'good first issue' or a documentation fix. Write the fix, add tests if applicable, follow the project's code style. Submit a clean PR with description.
milestones.criteria: PR follows CONTRIBUTING.md guidelines. Tests pass in CI. PR description clearly explains the change and why.

milestones.name: PR merged and next contribution planned
milestones.description: Address reviewer feedback, iterate until merged. Plan your next contribution: a larger feature, performance improvement, or new integration.
milestones.criteria: At least 1 PR merged. Positive interaction with maintainers. Next contribution area identified with a concrete plan.

open-source-contribution.connects_to: This is the culmination of your journey. Every project has prepared you to read and contribute to production AI code. Contributing to transformers uses knowledge from Projects 1-5. Contributing to vLLM uses Project 8 knowledge. Contributing to LangGraph uses Projects 12-13. This also builds the public technical credibility that strengthens your PM career.
